title: The Baby Race That Shocked Canada
summary: Imagine a contest where the winner is the mom who has the most babies! That's what happened in Canada almost 100 years ago. A rich man named Charles Vance Millar died in 1926. He was a prankster who loved jokes. In his will, he left money to the Toronto mother who gave birth to the most children in 10 years. The prize was worth about 9 million Canadian dollars today! The contest was called the Great Stork Derby. Newspapers followed the leading moms closely. One woman, Grace Bagnato, had 20 children. Another, Florence Brown, had 26. Brown said she didn't want an Italian woman to win. At that time, many people thought big families were bad. Birth control was illegal, and some people worried that poor or immigrant families were having too many kids. The derby sparked huge debates. Some people thought Millar was making fun of those ideas. The contest ended in 1936, and four women shared the money. It was a weird, wild story that showed how tough life was during the Great Depression.
why_it_matters: This shows that sometimes people make strange rules, and it reminds us that ideas about families and fairness have changed over time.

keywords.explanation: a person who likes to play funny tricks.
a legal paper that says who gets a person's money after they die.
a person who moves to a new country to live.
a hard time in the 1930s when many people were poor and had no jobs.
the name of the baby contest in Canada.
discussions where people argue different ideas.

questions.question: What was the prize in the Great Stork Derby?
How many children did Florence Brown have?
Why did some people worry about the contest?
options: Too many Italian women were winning
Immigrant families were having too many kids
questions.correct_answer: Immigrant families were having too many kids

questions.question: How many women shared the prize money?

background_read: Charles Vance Millar was a Canadian lawyer who liked playing jokes. He made this contest in his will.
The contest happened during the Great Depression, a time in the 1930s when many people had very little money and jobs were hard to find.
In those days, birth control was against the law in Canada, and some people thought only certain families should have many children.
Article_Structure: WHO: Charles Vance Millar and the mothers like Grace Bagnato and Florence Brown.
WHAT: A contest where the woman with the most babies in 10 years won money.
WHY: Millar wanted to make a point about social ideas about families and immigration.

perspectives.description: Some people thought the contest was a joke and showed how silly it was to judge which families should have children.
Others worried that the contest would make poor women have more children than they could feed.
The contest started big discussions about birth control and immigration that were important in Canada at that time.
